retrieved_docs.title: Excessive Gingival Display -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Some authors define a gummy smile as more than 3 to 4 mm of exposed gingival tissue in a smile, whereas others consider more than 2 mm of gingival exposure as excessive. [13] In general, a gum-to-lip distance of 4 mm or more during a smile is deemed "unattractive" by dentists. [14] Once we identify that a patient has excessive gingival exposure, the next step is to determine its etiology (Table 1). Table 1. Etiology of excessive gingival display is organized according to the affected tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- History and Physical -- Clinical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical findings of DIGO include the following: Firm, painless, nodular enlargement of the interdental papilla, limited to the keratinized portions of the gingiva and extending to the facial and lingual gingival margins. In severe cases, a large fold of hypertrophied gingival tissue is observed covering the crowns. If no secondary inflammation is present, it appears firm and pale pink with minute lobulations, pouting from underneath the gingival margin. This is delineated by a groove of tissue that does not bleed on touch. If secondary inflammation exists, the gingiva appears smooth and red or bluish-red. The enlargement is generalized but usually greater in the anterior regions. Typically, it is not seen in edentulous areas. [15] The gingival overgrowth disappears when teeth are extracted. [15]

retrieved_docs.content: Headaches, fatigue, visual problems, enlargement of the hands, feet, and tongue are features of acromegaly.

retrieved_docs.content: A compressive head dressing should be applied after the procedure and kept in place for 24 hours. In the recovery room, the nurses should know the ideal blood pressure parameters: systolic pressure <150 mm Hg and diastolic pressure >65 mm Hg. [33] [34] [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Excessive Gingival Display -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The critical element in managing a gummy smile is identifying its etiology, which determines the treatment plan and outcomes. A gummy smile may result from various disorders, including a short or hypermobile upper lip, altered passive eruption, vertical maxillary excess (VME), dentoalveolar extrusion, and gingival hyperplasia, but VME is 1 of its most common causes. [3] The investigations needed to diagnose a gummy smile, and its etiology include frontal and lateral facial analysis, static and dynamic lip analysis, lip line, interlabial distance, and display of incisors at rest, and complete periodontal examination. Oral radiographs and cephalometric analysis may also be needed; the latter is indicated for diagnosing VME. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Angular Chelitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The following are alterations in mouth structure leading to changes in lip approximation and increased salivary pooling and maceration at the labial commissures: The normal loss of skin turgor due to aging, smoking, or rapid weight loss Loss of vertical dimension of the face due to severe tooth wear, edentulous states, and mal-fitted dentures increasing overhang of the upper lip onto the lower one (overclosure) Retrognathic malocclusion Deepened furrowing of the skin dependent on the commissures (marionette lines) Conditions associated with enlarged lips such as oro-facial granulomatosis (OFG). Up to 20% of OFG patients suffer from AC, but Candida is usually not isolated from lesions Down syndrome: 25% of patients have AC due to macroglossia leading to tongue protrusion and drooling [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients may have had long-standing hypertension without even knowing it. After hypertension is confirmed on at least three different occasions based on the average of two or more readings taken at each of the subsequent visits, clinicians should assess the patient for evidence of end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, eyes, and kidneys. Most cases of hypertension are essential hypertension. However, secondary causes, such as renal artery stenosis, coarctation of the aorta, and/or metabolic disorder such as pheochromocytoma or hyperaldosteronism, hyperparathyroidism, hypothyroidism, as well as hyperthyroidism must be excluded. Sleep apnea syndrome has been linked to both pulmonary hypertension as well as systemic hypertension. Also, according to the Seventh Report of the Joint National Committee on Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Treatment of High Blood Pressure (JNC 7) guidelines, the patient should be assessed for cardiovascular risk factors other than hypertension, including metabolic syndrome, tobacco use, increased LDL-cholesterol, diabetes mellitus, obesity, family history of premature cardiovascular death, lack of exercise, and microalbuminuria. Social history should include the patient's use of over-the-counter medications since herbal medications such as licorice or ephedrine could cause left ventricular hypertrophy. The patient should also be asked about the use of oral contraceptives, excess alcohol intake, as well as the use of cardiac stimulants such as cocaine or amphetamines.

retrieved_docs.title: Excessive Gingival Display -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Excessive gingival display is considered an aesthetic problem. The cause of the excessive display determines the treatment. It is important to manage patients' expectations regarding treatment results. Patients must be aware of possible complications of treatment and consent to it.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnoses for DIGO include the following: False enlargement of gingival tissue: Pseudo-enlargement of the gingiva caused by an enlarged underlying bony tissue. The gingiva here is normal. Inflammation: Chronically inflamed gingival tissue is red or violaceous, smooth, and tends to bleed to the touch. Familial or hereditary conditions: Examples include familial fibromatosis, idiopathic fibromatosis, gingivomatosis, and hereditary gingival hyperplasia. The gingiva is pink and non-tender, involves the attached gingiva, the gingival margin, and the interdental papillae, and has a firm and leathery consistency. Physiological states: Puberty and pregnancy are associated with gingival enlargement. Scurvy: Vitamin C deficiency can produce very tender bleeding gingiva. Systemic diseases: leukemia, tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, as their diagnosis can be corroborated hematologically.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2754
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension, a common form of high blood pressure, is interpreted by the term ضغط الدم, the Arabic phrase that directly translates to “blood pressure,” highlighting the linguistic link between the clinical condition and its description.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1530
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are clinical manifestations of فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي (primary hypertension). An increase in blood pressure (زيادة ضغط الدم) is also associated with فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي. While فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي represents essential hypertension, it is conceptually linked to فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي (secondary hypertension) as part of the broader hypertension classification, distinguishing primary from secondary causes. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians recognize the hemodynamic signs of primary hypertension and differentiate it from secondary forms for appropriate evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 921
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is identified and interpreted through increased blood pressure measurements, which are central to its clinical assessment. Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis exhibits clinical features that are similar to those of essential hypertension, highlighting overlapping manifestations and the need to consider both conditions when evaluating patients with elevated blood pressure and related renal changes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, indicating that the severe renal vascular changes seen in malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis share comparable clinical manifestations, risk factors, and disease mechanisms with complicated primary hypertension and hypertension that arises secondary to other underlying conditions. This similarity underscores the importance of recognizing overlapping signs and symptoms when diagnosing and managing patients with advanced hypertensive organ damage and secondary causes of elevated blood pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base organizes pregnancy‑related maternal conditions under the umbrella term **Complications mainly related to pregnancy**, which is subdivided into the categories **Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium** and **Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium**. Specific parent concepts within this hierarchy include **Early or threatened labor**, **Hemorrhage during pregnancy** (e.g., abruptio placenta, placenta previa), **Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium**, and the broader “other conditions” group. **Cervical shortening in pregnancy** is classified as a child of both “Other conditions or status of the mother…” and “other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother,” and it is linked via an inverse‑isa relationship to **abnormalities of cervix (obstetric)**. Cervical shortening is further divided into trimester‑specific entities—**Cervical

Recognizing these interrelationships aids clinicians in categorizing, diagnosing, and managing the diverse complications that can arise during pregnancy, childbirth, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 153
retrieved_docs.content: the general complication class. Structural maternal findings such as cervical shortening in pregnancy and uterine size‑for‑dates discrepancy are children of “Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium.” Cervical shortening is further divided into second‑trimester, third‑trimester and unspecified‑trimester forms, each linked to “Early or threatened labor” and to “Short cervical length in pregnancy.” Stenosis of the cervix is also an “abnormalities of cervix (obstetric)” subtype. Bleeding presentations are captured by “Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester,” representing minor vaginal bleeding at any gestational stage. retrieved_docs.content: are considered indications for maternal care when pelvic‑organ abnormalities are known or suspected. **Uterine size for dates discrepancy** is a child of **Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium** and is also a type of **Finding related to pregnancy** that occurs during the **maternal pregnancy period**. Other findings in this period include **spotting in an unspecified trimester** (classified under early, first, or unspecified trimester hemorrhage), **false labor (مخاض كاذب)**, and **abnormal facial delivery findings** linked to **abnormal delivery (bevalling; abnormaal)**. Cardiovascular assessment in obstetrics emphasizes blood‑pressure measurement. The component **ضغط الدم (blood pressure)** is central to the diagnosis of **Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium**, while **ضغط الدم المنخفض (low blood pressure)** represents the opposite end of the spectrum. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 173
retrieved_docs.content: Complications mainly related to pregnancy encompass a broad spectrum of obstetric conditions that affect the mother during the maternal pregnancy period, childbirth, and the puerperium. These complications are organized under parent categories such as **Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium**, **Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium**, **Early or threatened labor**, **Hemorrhage during pregnancy** (including **abruptio placenta** and **placenta previa**), and **Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium**. Cervical pathology is a prominent component of this hierarchy. **Cervical shortening in pregnancy**—including the trimester‑specific forms **cervical shortening, second trimester**, **cervical shortening, third trimester**, and **cervical shortening, unspecified trimester** (synonymous with **short cervical length in pregnancy**)—is classified as an **abnormality of the cervix

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2927
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is a form of elevated arterial pressure that is identified and interpreted through measurement of ضغط الدم (blood pressure). The concept of ضغط الدم includes the entire spectrum of arterial pressures and is directly related to ضغط الدم المنخفض (low blood pressure), illustrating the continuum from hypotension to hypertension. Recognizing how essential hypertension is defined by ضغط الدم and how ضغط الدم connects with ضغط الدم المنخفض helps clinicians assess, diagnose, and manage blood pressure disorders more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1847
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension is interpreted as ضغط الدم (blood pressure), emphasizing that this complex form of high blood pressure is fundamentally a disorder of blood pressure regulation. ضغط الدم is directly related to ضغط الدم المنخفض (low blood pressure), indicating that the spectrum of blood pressure abnormalities—from elevated to reduced levels—are interconnected clinical entities. Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, reflecting that severe arteriolar damage in the kidneys can present with features indistinguishable from complicated or secondary forms of hypertension. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of evaluating both high and low blood pressure states and recognizing their potential overlap with serious renal vascular pathology such as malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis for accurate diagnosis and optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis and essential hypertension are clinically similar conditions, as both involve pathological changes in small arterial vessels that lead to elevated blood pressure and related end‑organ effects. Recognizing the overlap in their clinical presentations helps clinicians differentiate primary hypertension from secondary renal vascular disease, guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation, and informs management strategies aimed at controlling blood pressure and preserving kidney function.
